title: Employment/unemployment
paperName: Cambridge International A Level Economics (P3 & P4)
summary: 9708 A Level — types of unemployment, measurement, NAIRU, and AD–AS analysis.
learningObjectives: Define frictional, structural, and cyclical unemployment.
Explain NAIRU and Phillips curve links.

simpleExplanation.title: Labour market outcomes
simpleExplanation.summary: Cyclical unemployment rises in recessions when AD is deficient. Structural unemployment reflects skills and location mismatches.
steps: NAIRU: lowest sustainable rate without accelerating inflation.

flashcards.back: Short-term unemployment while workers search for jobs or firms search for workers — normal labour market turnover.
Mismatch between skills/location of workers and jobs available — persists even at full capacity.
Caused by insufficient AD in recession — falls when economy recovers.
Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment — lowest unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation.

flashcards.front: Labour force survey vs claimant count?
flashcards.back: LFS: ILO definition (actively seeking work); claimant count: those receiving unemployment benefit — LFS usually higher.
Cyclical: expansionary fiscal/monetary policy. Structural: retraining, education, relocation grants, supply-side reform.

sections.content: **Employment/unemployment** (9.3) links micro labour markets to **AD–AS**. Classify unemployment type before recommending policy — wrong diagnosis leads to ineffective policy.

items: **Short-run Phillips curve:** inverse relation between unemployment and inflation.
**Stagflation:** SRAS shifts left — unemployment and inflation rise together.
**Hidden unemployment:** discouraged workers, underemployment not in headline rate.

sections.question:
Unemployment in Country Z is 8% — above its estimated NAIRU of 5%. Inflation is 1.5% and falling. A large manufacturing sector has closed due to foreign competition.

(a) Identify the types of unemployment likely present.
(b) Evaluate whether expansionary monetary policy alone is sufficient to restore full employment. [12 marks]
sections.solution:
(a) **Types present:**
- **Cyclical:** 8% > NAIRU 5% → **3 percentage points** of demand-deficient unemployment — falling inflation confirms weak AD.
- **Structural:** Manufacturing closures from **foreign competition** → skills mismatch, regional job losses — workers may lack skills for new sectors.
- **Frictional:** Always present — workers between jobs.

(b) **Monetary policy case (expansionary):**
- **Lower interest rates** → ↑ I and C → **AD shifts right** → reduces **cyclical** unemployment toward NAIRU.
- Falling inflation gives **room to cut rates** without immediate overheating concern.

**Limitations:**
- **Structural unemployment** unaffected by AD — redundant manufacturing workers need **retraining**, not just lower rates.
- **Time lags** — monetary policy takes 12–18 months to work fully.
- **Liquidity trap risk** if rates already near zero — may need fiscal or supply-side support.
- Cutting rates may **weaken currency** — helps exporters but raises import prices (cost-push inflation risk).

**Judgement:** Expansionary monetary policy helps the **cyclical component** but is **insufficient alone** — needs **supply-side measures** (retraining, regional development) for structural unemployment.

steps.caption: Frictional, structural, cyclical unemployment definitions.
steps.embedHint: Cyclical linked to recession — output gap.

steps.caption: Labour force survey vs claimant count measures.
NAIRU — unemployment below which inflation accelerates.
AD deficiency → cyclical unemployment on AD–AS.
